Europe

Post-Roman — illuminated manuscripts to post-impressionism.
← All regions

A thousand years of churches, altarpieces, and — eventually — secular portraits, landscapes, and the individual human strangeness at the turn of the 20th century.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.
Book of Kells
Insular Celtic · c. 800 CE · Ireland
A Latin gospel book from monks on Iona and Kells. The ornament is so intricate that Gerald of Wales thought it "the work of an angel, not of a man."
Chartres Cathedral
High Gothic · 1194–1220 · France
Rebuilt in 26 years after a fire. Its stained glass — especially the "Chartres blue" — has never been matched; the recipe was lost.
The Arnolfini Portrait
Early Netherlandish · Jan van Eyck · 1434
A merchant and his wife in a Bruges interior. The convex mirror on the back wall reflects two more figures — one of them possibly the painter.
The School of Athens
High Renaissance · Raphael · 1509–1511
Plato and Aristotle at the center, surrounded by the Greek philosophical tradition. Raphael slipped portraits of himself and Michelangelo into the crowd.
David
High Renaissance · Michelangelo · 1501–1504
Seventeen feet of marble carved from a block others had given up on. David faces Goliath before the stone flies — tense, not triumphant.
Sistine Chapel Ceiling
High Renaissance · Michelangelo · 1508–1512
Four years, mostly alone, on scaffolding. Michelangelo considered himself a sculptor, not a painter, and complained about the job the whole way through.
The Night Watch
Dutch Golden Age · Rembrandt · 1642
A militia portrait transformed into a theatrical scene — figures stepping forward, a drumbeat, a girl glowing in the crowd. Not actually set at night.
Las Meninas
Spanish Golden Age · Velázquez · 1656
The Infanta and her attendants — but who is the painting for? The king and queen appear only in the mirror. Foucault wrote an essay on that mirror.
Girl with a Pearl Earring
Dutch Golden Age · Vermeer · c. 1665
Not a portrait but a "tronie" — a study of an imagined figure. The pearl itself is two quick strokes of white paint. We don't know who she was.
The Starry Night
Post-Impressionism · Van Gogh · 1889
Painted from the window of his asylum room at Saint-Rémy, from memory. The village below is imagined; the swirling sky he said he could actually see.
The Scream
Expressionism · Edvard Munch · 1893
Munch wrote in his diary: "I sensed an infinite scream passing through nature." The sky's red may be from the Krakatoa eruption ten years earlier.
Bayeux Tapestry
Norman · c. 1070 · France
Seventy meters of embroidered linen telling the Norman conquest of England. Probably stitched by English nuns for the new conqueror's half-brother.
Ghent Altarpiece
Early Netherlandish · Jan van Eyck · 1432 · Belgium
Twelve folding panels of unmatched detail. Stolen seven times across its history; the Just Judges panel has been missing since 1934.
The Birth of Venus
Early Renaissance · Botticelli · c. 1485 · Florence
Venus rises from the sea on a shell, blown to shore by wind-gods. Painted on canvas when fresco was still the fashion — and kept hidden from Savonarola's fires.
The Last Supper
High Renaissance · Leonardo · 1495–1498 · Milan
Painted on a refectory wall in an experimental technique that began flaking within decades. The fragility is part of the legend.
The Garden of Earthly Delights
Early Netherlandish · Bosch · c. 1500 · Spain
A triptych of Eden, excess, and hell. The right panel is crowded with monsters nobody has fully decoded — including one with sheet music tattooed on a buttock.
Impression, Sunrise
Impressionism · Monet · 1872 · Le Havre
The painting that gave a movement its name — a hostile critic meant "impression" as an insult. Hazy harbor, red sun, no detail that isn't a brushstroke.
The Kiss
Vienna Secession · Klimt · 1907–1908
Gold leaf and oil on canvas. A couple fused inside a shimmering shroud of pattern — Klimt's answer to the new century.
Notre-Dame de Paris
Gothic · 1163–1345 · Paris
Cathedral of the kings of France. Half the fabric is original medieval, half is Viollet-le-Duc's 19th-century reimagining. The 2019 fire took the spire.
The Isenheim Altarpiece
Northern Renaissance · Grünewald · 1512–1516 · Alsace
Painted for a hospital for plague and ergotism. Christ's body on the cross is covered in exactly the sores the patients saw on their own.
Primavera
Early Renaissance · Botticelli · c. 1480 · Florence
Venus in a grove of citrus. The three Graces, Mercury, and the story of Chloris turning into Flora — a Medici wedding gift, decoded differently in every generation.
Pietà
High Renaissance · Michelangelo · 1498–1499 · Vatican
Carved by a 24-year-old. Mary is impossibly young — younger than her son — because, Michelangelo said, virtue preserves her. His only signed work.
A Sunday on La Grande Jatte
Post-Impressionism · Seurat · 1884–1886
Pointillism's founding canvas — ten feet wide, built entirely from tiny dots. Parisians on a Sunday afternoon, rendered like nothing before.
Water Lilies
Impressionism · Monet · 1896–1926 · Giverny
Two hundred and fifty paintings of the pond in his garden, late in life, eyes failing. The largest canvases wrap around the room at the Orangerie.
The Thinker
Modern · Rodin · 1880–1904
Originally a small figure above The Gates of Hell — Dante, looking down at his Inferno. Enlarged and cast on its own, it became a universal emblem of thought.
Wanderer above the Sea of Fog
German Romanticism · 1818
A man stands on a rocky peak, his back to us, looking into swirling cloud. Romanticism in one image — the sublime, and the lone self against it.
The Third of May 1808
Spanish Romanticism · 1814
A Madrid citizen, arms flung wide, faces a French firing squad in lantern-light. Painted as an accusation — art reporting on atrocity for the first time.
The Hay Wain
English Romanticism · 1821
A farm cart in a shallow stream on a summer afternoon. Nothing is happening. Constable turned rural quiet into monumental painting.
Le Déjeuner sur l'herbe
Early Modern · 1863
A nude woman picnics with two fully dressed men. The scandal wasn't the nudity — it was that she looks directly at the viewer, unashamed.
Olympia
Early Modern · 1863
A courtesan reclining nude, a Black maid bringing flowers, a cat raising its tail. The Salon hung it high to keep people from slashing it.
